ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for taro milk drink with taro balls; closest_parent 'taro-based food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents taro-based products; no specific code for taro milk drink with taro balls exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific taro milk beverage with taro ball toppings.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one 400 ml cup of taro milk with small taro balls, as typically served at Singaporean bubble tea and specialty drink stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Taro milk drink with taro balls — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Taro raw (FDC ID 170071), Whole milk (FDC ID 746782), Taro starch balls estimated from taro starch composition, and added sugar
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for taro milk with taro balls exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by component analysis: taro milk base (~60% whole milk, ~25% taro puree, ~10% sugar, ~5% water) combined with small taro balls (~15% of total weight, composed primarily of taro starch). Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro (Colocasia esculenta) — analogous estimate based on published GI values for taro and taro-based products; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for taro milk with taro balls as a composite drink. GI estimated at 53 based on: (1) published GI of taro (Colocasia esculenta) = 54 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); (2) dilution effect of milk (GI ~27-34) and added sugar (GI ~65) in the composite drink; weighted blended GI estimated at approximately 53, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 66.4) / 100 = 35.2 g (serving size 400g, carbohydrate_g per 100g = 16.6g, total carbs per serving = 66.4g).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg per 100g) with moderate potassium (~145 mg per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Taro milk with taro balls has a low sodium content and provides potassium from taro and milk, making it generally acceptable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions; however, the added sugar content should be monitored as excess sugar intake can indirectly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~35 g GL) and significant added sugar (~10 g sugar per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of approximately 53, the large serving size (400 ml) results in a high glycemic load of approximately 35 g per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this beverage, or request reduced sugar and smaller portion sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk (~1.7 g per 100g) and high added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from whole milk and the high added sugar content of this beverage are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and consider requesting reduced-sugar versions or substituting with low-fat milk alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro Milk with Small Taro Balls is a popular Taiwanese-origin milk-based beverage widely served in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of a creamy taro-flavoured milk base made from blended or powdered taro (Colocasia esculenta), whole milk, and sugar, served chilled over ice and topped with small chewy taro balls (also called taro pearls or mini taro yuan). The taro balls are made from taro starch dough, cooked until soft and chewy with a slightly dense, glutinous texture similar to mochi. The drink has a naturally sweet, earthy, and mildly nutty taro flavour with a rich, creamy mouthfeel from the milk. The small taro balls add a satisfying chewy contrast to the smooth liquid base. Nutritionally, this beverage provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100g), primarily from carbohydrates (16.5 g per 100g) including added sugars (~10 g per 100g), with modest protein (2.4 g) from milk and small amounts of fat (2.8 g). The taro component contributes dietary fibre, potassium, and small amounts of vitamins B6 and C. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 53 (low GI), but the glycemic load per standard 400 ml serving is high (~35 g), making this drink a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or blood glucose levels. The beverage contains milk and is therefore not suitable for those with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly. In Singapore, this drink is commonly found at bubble tea shops, Taiwanese dessert stalls, and specialty beverage chains such as Happy Cup.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for taro milk with small taro balls was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for taro (FDC ID 170071) and whole milk (FDC ID 746782), with taro starch ball composition estimated from taro starch data. GI was estimated from published taro GI values (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability in commercial preparation methods.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central approved sources; no exact composite entry exists for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite beverage in the source database. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — no hydrogenated fats used in standard preparation). The glycemic index is set to 53 based on analogous estimate from taro GI data (University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. Serving size of 400g (approximately 400 ml) is used as the standard commercial serving for this beverage in Singapore. The description from the source restaurant notes there is no less-ice option; if less ice is requested, regular ice is substituted, which does not materially affect nutritional values. This record was sourced from field survey data (Happy Cup - Funan) and processed under Project Veritas v2.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 Singaporean cuisine requirement.
Estimated per_100g nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central component data for taro and whole milk, with taro ball starch composition.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes field.
Set glycemic_index=53 (analogous estimate from taro GI, University of Sydney GI Database) with gi_category='low' per ≤55 threshold rule.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving=35.2 based on 400g serving size.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Confirmed trans_fat_g=0.0 is justified (no hydrogenated fats in standard preparation) and documented in notes.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: closest_parent and none.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name.
